questions.question: For a woman at high risk (BRCA positive), at what age should annual screening mammography generally begin?
questions.detail: For high-risk women, screening mammography is highly appropriate starting at age 25–30 or 10 years before the age of a first-degree relative's diagnosis.

questions.question: A patient received 20 Gy of chest radiation for lymphoma as a child. When should annual screening MRI begin?
choices: At age 25 or 8 years post-exposure (whichever is later)
Only if a lump is palpable
questions.detail: Women with a history of childhood chest radiation (≥ 20 Gy) qualify for annual MRI starting at age 25 or 8 years after radiation.

questions.question: In a woman with an average risk (< 15% lifetime risk), is a screening MRI appropriate?
choices: Only if the mammogram is dense
Only if she has breast pain
questions.detail: MRI is not appropriate (rated as a 3) for screening women with average risk.

questions.question: A woman has a personal history of breast cancer and a risk model showing 15–20% lifetime risk. What is the appropriateness of MRI?
questions.detail: For medium-risk women (15–20% risk), MRI is rated as a 7.

questions.question: True or False: In high-risk women, MRI should replace mammography.
questions.detail: Mammography and MRI are complementary examinations, and both should be performed in high-risk patients.

questions.question: For a transgender woman over age 50, screening mammography is recommended if they have been on hormones for at least:
questions.detail: Screening for transgender women is recommended if they are > 50 years old and have taken hormones (Estrogen/Progestin) for > 5 years.

questions.question: Screening mammography is recommended for transgender men if:
choices: They have had a complete bilateral mastectomy
They still have breast tissue (even after reduction)
questions.detail: Transgender men should have screening if they still have breast tissue.

questions.question: Which imaging is appropriate for a woman < 40 with cyclical, bilateral breast pain?
questions.detail: For cyclical pain in a woman < 40, no imaging is appropriate.

questions.question: For a woman < 40 with NON-cyclical breast pain, which modality 'might be appropriate'?
questions.detail: Ultrasound is the only modality that 'might be appropriate' (5) for non-cyclical pain in younger women.

questions.question: In a newly diagnosed Stage 1 breast cancer patient with no symptoms, what imaging is appropriate to rule out distal metastases?
questions.detail: No systemic imaging (CT, MRI, PET) is indicated for the initial workup of Stage 1 disease without symptoms.

questions.question: For surveillance to rule out local recurrence in a post-therapy patient, which is most appropriate?
questions.detail: Diagnostic mammography or tomo is highly appropriate (9) for surveillance.

questions.question: A 22-year-old male presents with an indeterminate palpable mass. What is the appropriate first imaging step?
questions.detail: For symptomatic males < 25, ultrasound is appropriate (8).

questions.question: A 40-year-old male presents with an indeterminate palpable mass. What is the appropriate first imaging step?
questions.detail: For males > 25, mammography is the preferred initial study.

questions.question: A male patient has a physical exam highly concerning for cancer (ulcerative mass, nodes). What is appropriate?
choices: Both Mammography (9) and Ultrasound (8) to stage
questions.detail: Highly suspicious exams in males require both mammo and ultrasound for staging.

questions.question: A woman > 40 presents with a palpable finding. What is the most appropriate first step?
questions.detail: Mammography is the standard first step for a palpable in a woman > 40.

questions.question: If a woman > 40 has a palpable finding but the mammogram is negative, what is the next step?
questions.detail: Even with a negative mammogram, a palpable in this age group requires ultrasound.

questions.question: For a woman < 30 with a palpable mass, what is the appropriate initial evaluation?
questions.detail: For women < 30, ultrasound is the first step.

questions.question: If a woman < 30 has a suspicious ultrasound, what is the next most appropriate step?
questions.detail: Core biopsy is the most appropriate (9) next step for a suspicious US in this age group.

questions.question: A woman < 30 has an ultrasound finding eligible for BI-RADS 3 (e.g., fibroadenoma). What is the follow-up?
choices: Short interval follow-up (usually Q6 months x 2 years)
questions.detail: BI-RADS 3 findings require short-interval follow-up for 2 years.

questions.question: In the 'Symptomatic Male Breast,' if history and exam are consistent with 'bitch tits' (pseudogynecomastia):
questions.detail: For clear pseudogynecomastia, no imaging is necessary.

questions.question: A woman > 40 has mammographic findings of a simple lipoma at the site of a palpable mass. What is the next step?
questions.detail: If the mammogram definitively identifies a benign lesion (lipoma) at the palpable site, no more imaging is needed.

questions.question: Which molecular subtype of breast cancer is most associated with BRCA1 mutations?
questions.detail: BRCA1 is more often associated with triple negative cancers.

questions.question: For a woman with a lifetime risk of 22%, which screening combination is 'highly appropriate'?
questions.detail: Lifetime risk > 20–25% warrants both mammography and MRI.

questions.question: In a transgender woman, which BMI increases breast cancer risk?
questions.detail: A BMI > 35 is noted to increase risk in transgender women.

questions.question: A woman between ages 30–39 presents for initial evaluation of a palpable. Both Mammo and US are rated as:
questions.detail: Both mammo and ultrasound are appropriate (8) for this age range.

questions.question: Which modality is generally faster and easier than stereotactic biopsy?
questions.detail: Ultrasound is faster and easier; if a mass is visible on US, biopsy should be done that way.

questions.question: What is the typical gauge for an automatic spring-loaded device used for breast masses?
questions.detail: A 14 gauge device is usually used for masses.

questions.question: To visualize the needle length better during ultrasound biopsy, where should the mass be placed?
choices: In the center of the screen
On the far side of the screen
On the near side of the screen
questions.detail: Placing the mass on the far side allows for better visualization of the needle length.

questions.question: Which needle orientation is preferred to avoid the 'embarrassing complication' of a pneumothorax?
questions.detail: The needle should be parallel to the chest wall to prevent pneumothorax.

questions.question: Where should the local anesthetic be placed for a small lesion?
choices: Right up to but not into the lesion
questions.detail: Anesthetic should be placed near the lesion but not inside it.

questions.question: When performing multiple passes on a mass, which part should you try to biopsy first?
questions.detail: Biopsying the deeper part first prevents bleeding from obscuring the view.

questions.question: If you have two lesions to biopsy, which should you target first to avoid obscuration from bleeding?
choices: The one closest to the skin
questions.detail: Hit the smaller one first, as a bleeding larger one is more likely to obscure the field.

questions.question: If a lesion is both solid and cystic, which component should be biopsied?
questions.detail: Always target the solid part of a complex lesion.

questions.question: You accidentally inject a bunch of air while giving lidocaine and now the mass is obscured. What is the next step?
choices: Try to see through the air with Doppler
Call the surgeon for an immediate OR excision
questions.detail: If air obscures the mass, you must reschedule.

questions.question: When biopsying an axillary lymph node, which part of the node should be targeted?
questions.detail: Target the cortex when biopsying a node.

questions.question: A suspicious hypoechoic mass turns out to be a debris-filled cyst upon aspiration. The fluid is non-bloody and the lesion disappears. What do you do?
choices: Pitch the fluid; no need for cytology
Place a clip and follow in 6 months
questions.detail: If a cyst is non-bloody and disappears, you are done.

questions.question: If you aspirate a cyst and the fluid is bloody, what is the next step?
choices: Send it to cytology and place a clip
questions.detail: Bloody fluid requires cytology and a clip.

questions.question: A cyst aspiration yields purulent fluid that 'smells like a zombie farted.' What is the next step?
choices: Send to microbiology lab for culture and sensitivity
questions.detail: Purulent fluid needs microbiology/culture.

questions.question: If a lesion does NOT disappear after aspiration of fluid, what is the next step?
choices: Proceed to core biopsy of the residual solid mass
questions.detail: A residual mass after aspiration requires core biopsy.

questions.question: How many samples are typically taken with a 10–11 gauge vacuum-assisted needle?
questions.detail: A 10–11 gauge needle typically takes 12 samples.

questions.question: If the breast compresses to less than what thickness, is a stereotactic biopsy contraindicated?
questions.detail: If the breast is < 20 mm compressed, you should do wire localization instead.

questions.question: What is the 'accordion effect' in the context of breast procedures?
choices: Clip migration after compression is released
questions.detail: The accordion effect refers to clip migration.

questions.question: After placing a biopsy marker, which imaging is needed to evaluate for migration?
questions.detail: An orthogonal mammogram is used to check clip placement.

questions.question: The 'Localization and Accuracy Test' for stereotactic systems is performed:
questions.detail: System alignment is checked daily.

questions.question: For a patient with breast implants, is a core biopsy contraindicated?
questions.detail: Implants are NOT a contraindication for core biopsy.

questions.question: Which size needle is small but can still 'burst a saline implant'?
questions.detail: Even a tiny 25 gauge needle can burst a saline implant.

questions.question: 'No Calcs in the core' from a biopsy of milk of calcium means you need to look at the cores with:
questions.detail: Polarized light is needed to see the birefringence of milk of calcium.

questions.question: In ultrasound-guided biopsy, how many passes do most texts recommend (even if diagnosis is often made in 1–2)?
questions.detail: Most texts recommend 5 passes.

questions.question: For stereotactic biopsy using a 7–9 gauge needle, how many samples are typically taken?
questions.detail: A 7–9 gauge needle requires only 4 samples.

questions.question: 'CXR with clips in the axilla of a woman' is a classic Aunt Minnie look for:
questions.detail: Axillary clips in a woman usually indicate prior breast cancer surgery.

questions.question: What does the acronym MQSA stand for?
questions.detail: It stands for the Mammography Quality Standards Act.

questions.question: Which agency is the 'Evil Overlord' (main regulator) behind MQSA?
questions.detail: The FDA is the body behind MQSA.

questions.question: How many months of dedicated mammography training are required during residency?
questions.detail: Three months of training are required.

questions.question: How many mammograms must a resident read during the last two years of training?
questions.detail: Residents must read 240 mammograms.

questions.question: According to MQSA, the 'recall rate' for a screening mammogram should be less than:
questions.detail: The recall rate must be under 10%.

questions.question: What is the 'appropriate target range' for the recall rate during a medical audit?
questions.detail: The target recall rate is 5–7%.

questions.question: How many cancers per 1000 screened is the appropriate target range?
questions.detail: The goal is to find 3–8 cancers per 1000.

questions.question: Facilities must provide patients with a written 'lay report' of results within how many days?
questions.detail: A lay summary must be given within 30 days.

questions.question: If a patient moves, can they obtain their 'original' mammograms from a facility?
choices: Yes, patients can obtain original mammograms
Only if a lawyer requests them
questions.detail: Patients are entitled to their original mammograms.

questions.question: Who is 'ultimately responsible' for the Quality Control program at a mammography facility?
questions.detail: The Interpreting Physician is responsible for QC.

questions.question: What is the required resolution for line pairs in the anode-to-cathode direction?
questions.detail: The required resolution is 13 lp/mm.

questions.question: To pass image quality testing using a phantom, how many 'fibers' must be visible?
questions.detail: At least 4 fibers must be seen.

questions.question: To pass phantom image quality, how many 'microcalcification clusters' and 'masses' must be shown?
questions.detail: The requirement is 3 clusters and 3 masses.

questions.question: What is the dose phantom thickness and glandularity?
questions.detail: The phantom is 4.2 cm thick with 50% glandularity.

questions.question: The phantom dose is supposed to be less than how many mGy per image (with a grid)?
questions.detail: The limit is 3 mGy per image.

questions.question: True or False: There are strict dose limits for an 'actual patient' during mammography.
questions.detail: There are no patient dose limits, only phantom dose limits.

questions.question: What is the 'typical' dose for a standard two-view (CC + MLO) screening of one breast?
choices: 2 mGy per view (4 mGy total for one breast)
questions.detail: Typical dose is about 2 mGy per view.

questions.question: How often must 'Processor QC' be performed?
questions.detail: Processor QC is a daily task.

questions.question: How often must 'Phantom Evaluation' be performed?
questions.detail: Phantom evaluation is done weekly.

questions.question: How often must a 'Compression Test' be performed?
questions.detail: The compression test is a semi-annual task.

questions.question: How often must a medical audit and outcome analysis be performed?
questions.detail: Audits are performed annually.

questions.question: For how many years must a facility keep original mammograms if they have prior films?
questions.detail: Films must be kept for 5 years (or 10 if no priors).

questions.question: The 'average' compressed breast in the real world is:
questions.detail: The typical compressed breast is 6 cm.

questions.question: True or False: Digital mammography uses a 'fixed dose' (similar to screen-film).
questions.detail: Digital systems do not use a fixed dose and can use as much (or little) as appropriate.

questions.question: Darkroom Cleanliness' is a QA task that must be performed:
questions.detail: Darkroom cleanliness is a daily requirement.

questions.question: In Scenario 1, the PNL is 9 cm on MLO but only 6 cm on CC. What is the next step?
questions.detail: This is a technical failure; the PNLs must be within 1 cm of each other.

questions.question: In Scenario 2a, a mass is seen on two views on an annual screening. What is the appropriate assessment?
questions.detail: A suspicious finding on a screener must be a BI-RADS 0.

questions.question: After a callback, the mass persists and is suspicious. You perform an ultrasound and see a shadowing, angry mass. What is the 'Next Step'?
questions.detail: If cancer is highly likely, you must scan the rest of the breast and axilla to stage it.

questions.question: In Scenario 3a, a mass is seen on CC only (an asymmetry). What is the BI-RADS assessment?
questions.detail: Findings seen in one view require a callback (BI-RADS 0) for spot compression.

questions.question: In Scenario 3b, after spot compression is applied, the 'mass' disappears and looks like normal tissue. What is the next step?
questions.detail: If the finding does not persist on spots, it is BI-RADS 1.

questions.question: According to the 'Oldest Trick in the Book,' a male with a palpable mass should never have an ultrasound before a:
questions.detail: You must get a mammogram first because gynecomastia can look like cancer on US.

questions.question: In Scenario 4b, a male's mammogram shows a 'flame-shaped' density under the nipple. What is the likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Flame-shaped retroareolar density is classic for gynecomastia.

questions.question: In Scenario 5a, a 70-year-old woman with a history of lumpectomy (no RT) has a scar that is 'increasing' in density. What is the assessment?
questions.detail: An increasingly dense scar is suspicious and needs callback.

questions.question: The spot views in Scenario 5b show 'pointy' calcifications near the scar. What are they called?
questions.detail: Pointy calcifications are fine pleomorphic.

questions.question: What is the final diagnosis for Scenario 5 (increasing scar density + fine pleomorphic calcs)?
questions.detail: These features are highly suggestive of local recurrence.

questions.question: A 'shrinking breast' on mammography is a buzzword for:
questions.detail: The 'shrinking breast' is a hallmark of ILC.

questions.question: If you see 'lines radiating to a single point' on mammography, you say:
questions.detail: Radiating lines indicate architectural distortion.

questions.question: Architectural distortion + Calcifications' makes you think of:
questions.detail: This combination suggests Invasive Ductal and Ductal Carcinoma In Situ.

questions.question: Architectural distortion WITHOUT calcifications' makes you think of:
questions.detail: ILC classically presents as architectural distortion without calcifications.

questions.question: The 'Stepladder Sign' on ultrasound and the 'Linguine Sign' on MRI both indicate:
questions.detail: These are buzzwords for intracapsular rupture.

questions.question: Residual Calcs in the Lumpectomy Bed' is a buzzword for:
questions.detail: Calcs in the tumor bed indicate recurrence.

questions.question: If a biopsy result is 'discordant' with suspicious imaging (e.g., BR-5), what is the mandatory next step?
questions.detail: Discordant findings require surgical biopsy.

questions.question: CXR with clips in the axilla of a man' is a classic look for:
questions.detail: In a male, axillary clips suggest melanoma surgery.

questions.question: True or False: Surgical scars should generally get LESS dense with time.
questions.detail: Scars should lighten; if they darken, worry about cancer.

questions.question: For the purpose of the exam, screening mammography:
choices: Should only be done every 3 years
questions.detail: On the boards, screening saves lives.

questions.question: A '95-year-old with low-grade DCIS' needs which step on a board exam?
questions.detail: Even very elderly patients with DCIS get a surgical consult.

questions.question: If you see 'thick Coopers ligaments' on a mammogram, you should think of:
questions.detail: Thickened ligaments are a sign of edema.

questions.question: Thick FUZZY coopers ligaments with normal skin' is an imaging sign of:
questions.detail: Fuzzy ligaments suggest motion blur.

questions.question: What is the 'most common' primary tumor to metastasize to the breast?
questions.detail: Melanoma (along with lymphoma) is a top metastasis source.

questions.question: BRCA2' mutations are more commonly seen in which patient group?
choices: Male breast cancer patients (more than BRCA1)
questions.detail: BRCA2 is more strongly associated with male breast cancer.
